title: The Feast of Tabernacles
subtitle: Jesus withdraws while his kinsmen urge him toward the world's glory

stillness: He did not wish to walk openly in Judea.
Before the world's glory, he withdrew.
Be still, then, and withdraw a little with him.
gospel: After these things Jesus walked in Galilee,
for he would not walk in Judea,
because the Jews sought to kill him.
Now the Jews' feast of Tabernacles was at hand.
His brethren therefore said to him:
Pass from here, and go into Judea,
that your disciples also may see the works that you do.
For no man does anything in secret,
and himself seeks to be known openly.
If you do these things, manifest yourself to the world.
My time is not yet come;
but your time is always ready.
behold: The feast of Tabernacles draws near,
when the children of Israel dwelt in tents seven days,
in memory of the tents in which their fathers dwelt,
when, led out of Egypt, they wandered in the desert.
Behold them keeping this feast in remembrance of the Lord's mercies —
they who were about to kill the Lord.
And behold his kinsmen, drawing near to him,
seeking his glory that they might share in it,
urging him to go up to Jerusalem, the head of the kingdom,
and there show the glory of his miracles, and make himself known.
'You work signs,' they say, 'but in hiding;
make yourself known, appear to men, that you may be praised by men.'
consider: His kinsmen, seeking human and worldly glory, did not faithfully believe in him,
judging him to be vainly glorious, a lover and craver of the praise of men.
For it is the mark of those greedy for empty glory
that whatever glorious thing is theirs, they parade it in public.
They supposed that the world's praise and favor were the fruit of his miracles.
But because they were suggesting an evil to him — to seek worldly glory —
the Lord refused, and would not yield to them,
to give us the example of not seeking glory of this kind.
'My time,' he said, 'is not yet come;
but your time is always ready.'
The worldly have their time of glory ready, for they love the same things the world loves;
what they seek, they always find.
But the saints, who seek spiritual glory, have no time ready here,
for they condemn what the world loves, and despise the world.
There are two glories, and so there are two feasts.
The worldly have temporal feasts — to revel, to feast, and such outward delights;
the saints have spiritual feasts, which stand in spiritual delights.
Many in this present life always want to keep the feast,
and therefore in the life to come they will keep the vigil.
They want now to be in carousing and drunkenness, in laughter and vanity;
and therefore they will be forever in hunger, and thirst, and weeping, and tribulation.
This life is a vigil: we ought to fast, and to weep over our sins,
that we may come to the feast of the heavenly homeland.
Blessed are they that mourn — behold the vigil; for they shall be comforted — behold the feast.
But vain men want to keep the feast here, and so they will come to the vigil:
Woe to you that are filled — behold the feast; for you shall hunger — behold the vigil.
This life is an exile, and foolish would be the pilgrim
who, set in exile, would wish to keep the feast,
but ought to wait until he returns to his own country —
and our country is the heavenly land, which awaits us.
prayer: grant me with heart and desire to go up to the festal day of the eternal solemnity,
and always to prepare myself for it,
that when the time of your visitation shall come,
I may deserve to arrive there happily, to contemplate you face to face.
I thirst and I long, wretched as I am, for your grace;
I come and I sigh to you, seeking it with my whole heart.
and to receive of it so abundantly
that the gifts of graces may both suffice for me
and flow from me to my neighbors through good will, and profit them;
so that you, seeing in me the work of mercy,
may be moved to pour a fuller grace upon me. Amen.

latinJewel.latin: Beati qui lugent, ecce vigilia, quoniam ipsi consolabuntur, ecce festum.
latinJewel.english: Blessed are they that mourn — behold the vigil; for they shall be comforted — behold the feast.
